title: Expand operating resources in high-growth differentiated areas
decisionQuestion: When should Microsoft expand operating expense and headcount instead of retaining savings or constraining resources?
situation: Microsoft entered FY2022 with strong growth opportunities and operating savings, requiring a choice between preserving cost restraint and reinvesting in differentiated growth areas.
objectives: Preserve confidence in long-term margin execution
conditions: The target area has observable high growth.
Microsoft offers differentiation customers care about.
Prior investment execution supports confidence in returns.
constraints: Operating investment must remain concentrated rather than uniform.
Growth in expense and headcount must be justified by opportunity and execution.

options.description: Retain operating savings and constrain headcount growth across the portfolio.
options.expectedBenefit: Maximizes near-term operating leverage and lowers execution risk.

options.description: Expand operating expense and headcount selectively in high-growth differentiated areas.
options.expectedBenefit: Captures growth where customer value and Microsoft differentiation are strongest.
options.principalRisk: Higher cost growth if opportunity or execution weakens.

rejectedBenefit: Retaining savings would improve near-term operating leverage.
observations: Amy Hood made allocation to the highest-growth places the operating-expense test.
The FY2022 plan contemplated headcount growth above 12 percent.
inferences: Cost discipline is conditional allocation discipline, not a permanent preference for absolute cuts.

evidenceSpans.exactQuote: are we continuing to invest it in the highest growth places?
evidenceSpans.speaker: Amy Hood

evidenceSpans.exactQuote: headcount growth would be 12 percent plus
evidenceSpans.speaker: Amy Hood
